q: Every Product Backlog Item should be created by the Product Owner personally and only then can the Development Team add details.
exp: False. The Product Owner is solely accountable for decisions in the Product Backlog, but the legwork of managing it might be fully delegated to the Development Team.

q: During Product Backlog Refinement, who is allowed to update the estimates on Product Backlog Items?
opts: The Product Owner and the Stakeholders
The Product Owner and the Development Team
Any member of the Scrum Team
exp: Product Backlog Refinement is a collaborative practice involving the Product Owner and the Development Team — these two groups update estimates.

q: What provides guidance to the Development Team on why it is building the Increment?
exp: The Sprint Goal is an objective set for the Sprint that provides guidance to the Development Team on why it is building the Increment.

q: The Scrum Master should refuse entry to the Sprint Planning if the Product Owner has not prepared a Sprint Goal in advance.
exp: False. The Scrum Team crafts the Sprint Goal collaboratively during Sprint Planning — the Product Owner does not need to arrive with one pre-made.

q: When a product grows large it is possible the PO will get help from others. Who remains accountable for the Product Backlog?
exp: The Product Owner remains accountable for the Product Backlog even when the day-to-day management is delegated to others.

q: The Product Owner can delegate Product Backlog management activities to the Development Team but remains accountable.
exp: True. The PO may delegate the legwork to the Development Team; however, the Product Owner remains solely accountable for the outcomes.

q: To whom should the Product Owner formally report in Scrum?
opts: No one — the PO is accountable to stakeholders but has no formal reporting line in Scrum
exp: Scrum does not prescribe a formal reporting line for the Product Owner. The PO is accountable to the organization and stakeholders for maximizing product value.

q: Who is responsible for ordering and prioritizing the Product Backlog?
exp: The Product Owner is solely responsible for ordering the Product Backlog to maximize the value of the work performed by the Scrum Team.

q: Which of the following statements is true about the Sprint Review?
opts: It is a formal status meeting where the team reports to management.
It is an informal working session where the Scrum Team and stakeholders inspect what was done and adapt the Product Backlog.
Only the Scrum Team is allowed to attend.
The Product Owner presents a formal demo to management.
exp: The Sprint Review is an informal working session. The Scrum Team and invited stakeholders inspect the Increment and collaborate on next steps for the Product Backlog.

q: Who has the authority to cancel a Sprint?
exp: Only the Product Owner has the authority to cancel a Sprint, though it is a drastic action typically taken only when the Sprint Goal becomes obsolete.

q: What are three key characteristics of the Product Owner role?
opts: Product Value Maximizer, Lead Facilitator of Key Stakeholder Involvement, Product Marketplace Expert
Sprint Planner, Team Manager, Budget Controller
Backlog Writer, Meeting Organizer, Risk Manager
Technical Lead, Architecture Reviewer, Code Approver
exp: The PO maximizes product value, facilitates key stakeholder involvement, and maintains expertise in the marketplace the product serves.

q: Who is the chief product visionary on a Scrum Team?
exp: The Product Owner is the chief product visionary — responsible for the Product Goal and maximizing the value of the product.

q: True or False: The Product Owner has enough authority to cancel a Sprint even if the Development Team disagrees.
exp: True. The Product Owner is the only person who can cancel a Sprint. No one else can override this authority.

q: What does a Burn-down Chart track?
opts: Hierarchy of tasks in a project
How much work remains until the end of the Sprint or Release
Dependencies and start/stop times for project tasks
exp: A Burn-down Chart tracks remaining work vs. time. Scrum does not mandate it but it is a widely used practice to visualize Sprint progress.

q: How frequently should a product be released to optimize value?
opts: Only when all planned features are complete
Frequently enough to elicit feedback and validate hypotheses
Only when the Product Owner approves a major milestone
exp: Releases should be frequent enough to gather meaningful stakeholder feedback and allow the team to validate hypotheses about the product.
